coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as electron “bridge” in heterostructure of alpha-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 for efficient degradation of indole using heterogeneous photo-Fenton
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xuehua
Ge, Fuxiang
Ding, Hui
Zhou, Xiaotong
Li, Xiaobing
coredata.dc:description: A special component, acting as “bridge” in composite photocatalyst, can remarkably improve the transfer and migration of charges by strengthening interfacial contact and constructing conductive channels. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) may be suitable for the bridge due to great conductivity, sufficient surface groups and more delocalized electrons. Here, we fabricated a composite of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 for heterogeneous photo-Fenton degradation of indole and investigated the “bridge” roles of NCDs. Although the introduction of NCDs reduced surficial adsorption and light absorption abilities, the composite has a significant improvement in photocurrent density and EIS spectra, which were attributed to the bridge of NCDs between α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 through Fe-O-C and N-CN bonds. Besides, great conductivity of NCDs is another important factor for the transfer channels of charges. Finally, a Z-scheme mechanism was proposed for explanation of the transfer and migration of charges.

(a, b) TEM images of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, (c) TEM image of NCDs, (d)SEM images of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, and (e-h) corresponding EDX mapping.

(a) XPS survey spectrum of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, the high-resolution binding energy spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) Fe 2p, and (f) FTIR spectra of NCDs, g-C3N4, α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4.
(a, b) Nitrogen sorption isotherms of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, respectively, and corresponding pore size distributions (inset).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of NCDs, g-C3N4, α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4.
Indole degradation profile under different conditions (a) and with different photocatalysts (b) (C0 =50 mg/L, initial amount =100 ml, catalysts =10 g/L, H2O2 =90 mmol/L, Xenon lamp P = 350 W).

Effects of removal experimental conditions on degardation efficiency of indole, (a) photocatalyst amount (C0 =30 mg/L, H2O2 =90 mmol/L, pH=5), (b) H2O2 dosage (C0 =50 mg/L, catalysts=20 mg/L, pH=3.5), (c) initial contaminant concentration (catalysts=20 mg/L, H2O2 =90 mmol/L, pH=5), (d) pH value (C0 = 50 mg/L, catalysts = 0 mg/L, H2O2 =90 mmol/L,).
(a) Transient photocurrents and (b) EIS spectra of g-C3N4, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4.

(a) Degradation of contaminant over α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 with the addition of different radical scavengers, and (b) ESR spectra of DMPO-•OH adducts in aqueous solution before and after 5 min under light irradiation.
The proposed heterogeneous photo-Fenton mechanism of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4.
(a) The reusability of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 for degradation of contaminant and (b) XRD patterns of the α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 before and after cycling photocatalytic experiments.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as electron “bridge” in heterostructure of alpha-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 for efficient degradation of indole using heterogeneous photo-Fenton

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A special component, acting as “bridge” in composite photocatalyst, can remarkably improve the transfer and migration of charges by strengthening interfacial contact and constructing conductive channels. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) may be suitable for the bridge due to great conductivity, sufficient surface groups and more delocalized electrons. Here, we fabricated a composite of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 for heterogeneous photo-Fenton degradation of indole and investigated the “bridge” roles of NCDs. Although the introduction of NCDs reduced surficial adsorption and light absorption abilities, the composite has a significant improvement in photocurrent density and EIS spectra, which were attributed to the bridge of NCDs between α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 through Fe-O-C and N-CN bonds. Besides, great conductivity of NCDs is another important factor for the transfer channels of charges. Finally, a Z-scheme mechanism was proposed for explanation of the transfer and migration of charges.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation offer a green and sustainable approach to remove organic pollutants due to minimal environmental impact, energy consumption and economic cost [1,2]. As well known, the efficiency of photocatalyst highly depends on ability of harvesting light and photo-induced electron or hole (charges) transfer and migration. A mainstream strategy to improve photocatalytic performance is constructing heterojunction structure through comprising multiple components with matched energy level [2]. However, these heterostructures usually suffer from weak interfacial contact due to lattice mismatch, interfacial boundaries or defects and so on, leading to poor interfacial transfer and migration of charges. Besides, given migration of charges from a higher energy position to lower one, energy barriers of offsets between adjacent components may not favor a desirable transfer direction, even result in an adverse consequence [3]. Thus, special components acting as charge “bridges” have attracted increasing attentions to overcome these drawbacks by strengthening interfacial contact, heightening charges transferring and migrating transfer direction. Noble metal (like Pd and Au) nanoparticles are usually adopted as “bridge” owing to its small size, great conductivity and good bonding potentials with adjacent components, but the high price and complicated synthesis process restrict its applications [4]. Hence, it is highly desirable for inexpensive and facile materials as the “bridges” for improvement of composite photocatalyst.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimension carbon nanomaterials with size of less 10 nm, which usually possess carbon core with sp2 hybridized structure and rich hydroxyl and carboxylic surface groups [5,6]. The sp2 hybridized carbon endow CDs with outstanding conductivity through delocalized π electrons, and rich surface groups supply many access to form chemical bonds with adjacent component [7]. Moreover, heteroatom doping can further improve the performance of CDs [8,9], and nitrogen is the most widely used due to its lowing work function ability and supplying more electron to carbon network [10]. Thus, nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) is a desirable candidate as charges “bridge” in composite heterostructures. Besides, N-doped method are used to stabilize metal atom in carbon network by coordinating and binding with metal species, which strengthen contact of carbon materials and metal active sites. In photocatalysis, CDs are usually used as photosensitizer and electron reservoir to enhance performance [11,12]. In fact, most CDs have weak visible absorption, but strong absorption in ultraviolet light, which is only 8% in solar spectrum. Nevertheless, a majority of literatures usually highlights and discusses both effects at the same time [13]. Distinguishing which is dominant in photocatalytic process is of significance to determine the direction for enhancing performance and choosing appropriate synthesis method. What’s more, further work needs to be done to strengthen interfacial contacts by using CDs as charges “bridge” in composite photocatalysts.

Indole is a typical recalcitrant aromatic nitrogen heterocyclic compound, consisting of a five-membered nitrogen-containing pyrrole ring fused to a six-membered benzene ring, which usually exists in coal chemical wastewater [14]. Due to their stable chemical structure, indole cannot be effectively removed in conventional wastewater treatment process, once discharged into the natural environments, they could cause irreversible damage to cells by accumulating in organisms, resulting in teratogenicity, carcinogenesis and mutagenesis and other adverse effects on animals and human beings [15]. Therefore, it is essential to develop advanced technique to remove indole in wastewater. The biodegradation of indole has been widely investigated in recent years, but it usually takes several days or weeks to remedy indole from aqueous solutions [15].

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) is an efficient approach to remove indole by reactive oxygen species (ROS), and Fenton is typical AOPs by generating ROS of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) via ferrous ions activating hydrogen peroxide. Due to high oxidation-reduction potential of + 2.80 V, •OH can non-selectively remove almost organic pollutant. However, acidic aqueous-based medium (pH=2.5–4) and huge ferric hydroxide sludge dramatically increase cost of degradation. Heterogeneous Fenton, using immobilized solid iron species (Fe) instead of iron ion, not only prevent the generation of sludge due to iron leaching, but also widen the pH range of application. Unfortunately, the solid iron have a lower rate of regeneration of Fe(Ⅱ) than iron ion [16], and how to accelerate the regeneration of Fe(Ⅱ) become a crucial issue for heterogeneous Fenton. At present, heterogeneous photo-Fenton, using photo-induced electron to accelerate the regeneration of Fe(Ⅱ), has attracted increasing attention due to environmental friendliness and sustainability [17], and construction of photocatalyst with heterojunction structure is a mainstream approach due to wide light absorption region and low charge recombination. Alpha-Fe2O3, as one n-type semiconductor, can absorb visible light owing to inherent band gap of 2.2 eV, and it is common candidate for composite photocatalyst of heterogeneous photo-Fenton, as solid iron source [18]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is another famous semiconductor with visible light absorption ability, which is also applied in heterogeneous photo-Fenton reaction [19]. Fortunately, the conduction and valence band of both materials match very well for construction of Type Ⅱ or Z-scheme heterostructure, which further promote photo-induced charges transfer and separation and finally improve photocatalytic efficiency [20,21]. Recently, the composite photocatalysts combining α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 have been successfully fabricated and applied in water splitting [20,21], carbon dioxide reduction [22], photochemical reaction [23], and heavy metal ions reduction [24], showing great performance. Moreover, there are rare reports about using composite α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 as catalysts of heterogeneous photo-Fenton.

Hence, we selected α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 as main components to fabricate composite α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 for heterogeneous photo-Fenton degradation of indole and innovatively introduced NCDs as “bridge” between the interfaces to improve the charge transfer and migration. Moreover, the specific role of NCDs in the composite photocatalyst was investigated in details by various techniques. The characterized results show that NCDs not only reduced the specific surface area and porous diameter of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 due to the covering of NCDs, but also decreased absorption of visible light owing to its ultraviolet photosensitivity. Interestingly, the photocurrent density of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 has a significant improvement, which is 18.93 and 2.37 times higher than that of g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4, respectively. Moreover, EIS spectra also indicate its lowest charge transfer resistance in all samples. These improvements were attributed to the introduction of NCDs, which linked or “bridge” α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 through chemical bonds, such as Fe-O-C and N-CN. This bridging chemical bonds and great conductivity of NCDs constructed new channels for the transfer and migration of photo-induced charges between the interfaces of different components. Based on ROS trapping experiments and energy bonds position analysis, a Z-scheme type heterojunction structure was proposed to deduce the mechanisms of photo-induced charge transfer and migration and accelerating the regeneration of Fe(Ⅱ) in the heterogeneous photocatalyst. This work may open up direction for using CDs or NCDs as charge “bridge” in composite catalysts.

A classical hydrothermal approach was used to prepare NCDs. Typically, 3 g citric acid and 2 g urea were dissolved in 15 ml of distilled water. After stirring, the solution was sealed in Teflon-lined autoclave and then was held for 5 h at a temperature of 180 °C in a drying oven. After centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove precipitates or big fragments, the supernatant was obtained as the solution of NCDs. The solid NCDs was obtained by drying the supernatant at 70 °C for 12 h in low vacuum environment.

The g-C3N4 was prepared by pyrolysis approach. 5 g melamine were heated in a tube furnace from room temperature to 520 °C at a rate of 20 °C min−1. After maintained for 2 h at the temperature, the sample was cooled down to room temperature. Finally, the as-prepared yellow solid was milled into g-C3N4 powder.

The sample of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 were fabricated by first hydrothermal combination and then calcining consolidation. In a typical procedure, 0.2 g of NCDs, 0.2 g of g-C3N4 and 0.6 g FeCl•6 H2O were dissolved in 50 ml water, and the solution was heated for 6 h at a temperature of 180 °C in the autoclave. Then, the solid composites were separated from the solution by centrifuging at 3000 rpm for 20 min. After cleaned three times by distilled water, the solid were calcined at 520 °C for 2 h with nitrogen protection to obtain the sample of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4. Using similar procedure, various photocatalysts were prepared with different mass ratio of NCDs, α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4.

The images of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were obtained on a JEM-2100 electron microscope (Jeol, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Panalytical X′Pert PRO system using Cu-Kα radiation. Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) was obtained on a NETZSCH STA449F3 apparatus with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from 35 to 600 °C under air atmosphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were carried out on X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (PHI 5000 Versa Probe, UIVAC-PHI, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured on Nicolet 5700 spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA) in range of 400–4000 cm−1. Ultraviolet visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were measured on a Cary 300 spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan) with different excitation wavelengths. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was obtained Thermo escalab 250Xi (Thermo fisher) with an unfiltered HeI gas discharge lamp.

The heterogeneous photo-Fenton degradation efficiency was measured under visible light using indole as a target contaminant. In a typical experiment, the photocatalyst of 10 mg were added to 100 ml indole solution (50 mg/L), and the solution were stirred for 30 min in darkness to reach adsorption equilibrium. Under irradiation by a 350 W Xenon lamp, H2O2 of 45 mM was added in the solution. Afterwards, 3 ml solution was extracted at certain interval of 10 min to analyze the residue content of indole in solution. Control experiments were conducted under the same steps using an equivalent dosage of bare α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 as catalysts. For recycle tests, the catalysts were separated by centrifugal, washed successively by ethanol and distilled water and reused in next run under the same conditions. All processes were carried out under magnetic stirring and room temperature. The content of indole in solution was measured by absorption value at wavelength of 269 nm.

The transient photocurrent response curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves were measured by electrochemical workstation (Chenhua CHI 760E). The Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared as following methods. 10 mg of photocatalyst were added into 1 ml of 0.5% nafion solution, which was subsequently treated by ultrasonication for 10 min. The dispersed solution was dropped on a fluorine-doped tinoxide glass (with size of
1 × 1
cm
2
) and dried at 50 °C for 4 h in low vacuum atmosphere. Then, the obtained electrode was inserted in the electrolyte of 0.1 mol/ L Na2SO4 solution. Under irradiation of 350 W Xenon lamp, the photocurrent response curves were measured at a bias potential of 0.1 V and with a given irradiation intervals of 30 s. The EIS was performed on an applied voltage of 0 V with an amplitude of 5 mV over a frequency between 10−2 and 105 Hz.

The identification of ROS was carried out by quenching experiment and electron spin resonance spectrometer (ESR, EMXmicro-6/1/P/L, Bruker). Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), triethanolamine (TEOA), and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were used as quenching scavengers of •OH, h +, and •O2
, respectively. In quenching experiment, these agents were added in solution, respectively, and its influence on degradation were examined every 10 min. During ESR measurement, 5,5-dimethy-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as spin-trapped reagent. After being reacted for 5 min, the partial samples were collected from the reaction solution, and immediately mixed with 0.2 M DMPO to form DMPO-radicals adduct, which was further measured by ESR equipment.

XRD patterns were characterize to investigate crystal structures of NCDs, g-C3N4, α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 1(a), NCDs present a broad and less intense peak in range of 22° to 25.5°, corresponding to (002) plane of graphitic structure, which is indicative of a larger interlayer spacing induced by the attachment of oxygen or nitrogen functional groups [25]. Moreover, CDs prepared by hydrothermal method usually possess amorphous structure owing to the absence of high temperature graphitization (>300 °C). For pure g-C3N4, characteristic diffraction peak at 13.2° in the XRD pattern is indexed to the (100) diffraction plane, corresponding to in-plane tri-s-thiazine repeating motifs. Furthermore, the prominent peak located at 27.5° are assigned to characteristic plane of (002), which corresponds to the interlayer stacking of conjugated aromatic systems [26]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of iron oxide are present at 2θ = 24.3°, 33.3°, 35.9°, 41.0°, 49.6°, 54.3°, 62.6° and 64.1°, which correspond to (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (214) and (300) diffraction planes of α-Fe2O3 [27], indicating that the iron oxide is hematite. When two or three components were combined, the crystal phases of α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4 did not change according to XRD patterns of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 composites, in which characteristic diffraction peaks of both g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3 appeared simultaneously. The diffraction signals of α-Fe2O3 are less intensity because of the existence of a very small amount of α-Fe2O3 in the composite of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4. It is to be stressed that the characteristic peak located at 13.2° (100) for g-C3N4 decreased in the sample of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and disappeared in the sample of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, respectively, which are attributed to the loading of α-Fe2O3 and NCDs on g-C3N4, partly weakening its presence. The absence of diffraction peak of NCDs is ascribed to amorphous structure and its low content in the composite of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4. It is indicative that the composite of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 has been successfully combined.

Raman analysis were performed to further study the structure properties of all samples, and the results were illustrated in Fig. 1(b). Raman spectrum of NCDs presents two characteristic bands of the famous D (1347 cm−1) and G (1578 cm−1), which corresponded to the disorder graphitic structure arising out of sp3 bonds from amorphous carbon species and in-plane vibrations of the sp2 hybridized carbon atom [25], respectively. Several characteristic peaks of pure g-C3N4 were observed at 707, 752 and 1233 cm−1. In addition, there were other very weak peaks in range of 1200–1600 cm−1, which are attributed to the vibration modes of CN heterocycles. The characteristic bands of α-Fe2O3 at 219, 282 and 390 cm−1 were evidently shown in the spectra of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 composites, corresponding to the vibration modes of A1 g, E1 g and E1 g [28], respectively. For the composite of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, the absence of characteristic peaks belonging to g-C3N4 is ascribed to the coverage of α-Fe2O3 and NCDs on surface. On the other hand, the characteristic peaks in range of 1200–1600 cm−1 are coincidence with the D and G bands of NCDs, leading to weaken the signals of g-C3N4. Both XRD and Raman results are indicative that the composite catalysts have been successfully prepared.

The TEM and SEM images of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 were taken and displayed in Fig. 2, respectively. Due to g-C3N4 with a typical two-dimensional sheet structure, the sample have a similar morphology of sheet (as shown in Fig. 2(a, b)), in where the α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles and NCDs were loaded on the sheet. It is easily observed that the as-prepared NCDs have torispherical shape and distribute uniformly without aggregation in Fig. 2(c). The distinct lattice spacing of NCDs are about 0.22 nm from HRTEM image (inset), corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic structure [25]. The SEM images (Fig. 2(d-h)) show the layered structure of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, suggesting the layered g-C3N4 loads some amount of α-Fe2O3, which is well agreement with the results of TEM. Elements distribution of the α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 were measured by SEM technique and corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mapping. As shown in Fig. 2(e), except of the part covered by α-Fe2O3 particles, element C almost distribute on all observed region, which was ascribed to coefficient contribution of NCDs and g-C3N4. Fig. 2(f) shows that the intensity of O elements was lower than that of C due to less amount in the composite. However, it distributed wider region than C element, which was owing to the contribution of α-Fe2O3. The contour of element N distribution in Fig. 2(g) images was similar with that of element Fe in Fig. 2(h), which was attributed that the bulged α-Fe2O3 were covered by NCDs. In addition, the N element shows a higher intensity in absent area of α-Fe2O3.

To deeply evaluate the binding behaviors of elements and surface groups, XPS was employed to characterize composite photocatalysts. Fig. 3(a) reveals that the survey spectra of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 show characteristic peaks around at 285, 530, 399 and 723 eV, corresponding C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Fe 2p peaks, respectively. These results are indicative of C, O, N, and Fe elements existing on surface of the composites. As shown in the high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 3.(b)), both composites show two similar deconvoluted peaks located at 284.6 and 286.3 eV, which are assigned to C-C bonding of sp2-hybridized carbon and C-N bonding groups. The difference is the characteristic peak at 288.1 eV corresponding to sp2-hybridized bonding of N-CN bonds for composite α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 [29], but the characteristic peak for α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 shifted to 288.9 eV, which should be ascribed to C-O bonds and caused by the calcination process [30]. The N 1 s spectra of both composites also could be deconvoluted into three similar peaks located about at 398.1, 399.2 and 400.1 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic nitrogen (C-NC), pyrrolic nitrogen (C-N-C) and quaternary nitrogen (N-(C)3), respectively (Fig. 3(c)) [28,31]. Fig. 3(d) gives the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum, which have three deconvolution peaks. The characteristic peaks at 531.9 and 532.8 eV are attributed to oxygen bonded with surface carbon atoms or surface -OH groups/adsorbed water molecule, and the peak at 530.7 eV is assigned to Fe-C-O bond. The existence of Fe-C-O indicated the strong interaction between α-Fe2O3 moiety and g-C3N4 moiety, which might serve as an electron pathway to facilitate electron transfer and migration between interfaces [30]. In high-resolution spectra of Fe 2p (Fig. 3(e)), both composites show similar properties, in which two obvious peaks at 710.6 and 724.5 eV correspond to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. In addition, the binding energy of both peaks are fully consistent with typical values of Fe3+ in α-Fe2O3 [28], indicating the formation of α-Fe2O3. Both XPS spectra of O 1 s and Fe 2p indicate the presence of α-Fe2O3, which are well agreement with the results of XRD. In composite catalyst, the strong interaction between the active component and the carrier will cause the transfer of electrons between them, which cause a shift in binding energy. If the active component transfers electrons to the carrier, its binding energy increases, and vice versa decreases. In the composites of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, the binding energy shifting to high location reveal the change of external electron density of O 1 s and C 1 s, leading to the movement of electron in NCDs, α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4. In summary, these results prove that the composite α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 were successfully fabricated.

The FTIR technology was applied to verify the functional groups and composition of NCDs, g-C3N4 α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, and the spectra were presented in Fig. 3(f). It is obviously observed that NCDs possessed abundant oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups such as stretching vibrations of N-H/O-H (3400–3000 cm−1), bending vibrations of N-H (1568 cm−1) and vibration of C-N (1238 cm−1), which is indicative of the successful doping of N atoms into CDs with plenty functional groups. For the pure g-C3N4, the sharp peak at 806 cm−1 originate from the breathing mode of the triazine unit. Several peaks at 1242, 1323, 1407 and 1454 cm−1 in the region of 1200–1500 cm−1 are ascribed to the typical stretching modes of the C-N heterocycles of g-C3N4, and the peak at 1647 cm−1 corresponds to CN stretching vibration [32]. The broad absorption band located in range of 3300–2900 cm−1 is assigned to N-H stretching vibration. Similar FTIR absorption bands also appeared in the spectra of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 composites, mainly in range of 3400–3000 cm−1 and 1200–1500 cm−1. These results illustrate that both composites maintain the same polycondensation structure as pure g-C3N4, which is in great agreement with XRD analysis. The characteristic peaks of NCDs were not obviously observed, which was due to its merging with dominant g-C3N4.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm were measured to investigate the porous properties and specific surface area of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 4(a, b), both composite show a type Ⅳ with a H3 hysteresis loop according to the IUPAC classification [23], indicating the existence of mesoporous structure in the samples. The surface area of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 were calculated to be 25.16 m2/g and 28.75 m2/g, respectively. Moreover, the average pore diameter of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 was calculated to be 7.0 nm, which is also lower than 10.2 nm of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4. The small NCDs were attached or deposited on the sample of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4, and it would block part of the pores between α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, leading to the decrease of the surface area and pore diameter. The smaller surface area and pore size are inevitably harmful for the adsorption of contaminant molecules on active sites and its transmissions in pores, respectively. Thus, the introduction of NCDs limited improvement of heterogeneous photo-Fenton degradation in adsorption and transmission of contaminants.

As a photocatalysts, the harvesting ability in visible light range play an important role in improving photocatalytic activity (degradation in current work), because the visible light account for a nearly half part in solar spectrum. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy of NCDs, g-C3N4, α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 were measured and shown in Fig. 5. NCDs have a broad UV absorption peaks in range of 250–310 nm, which is related to the electron transitions from π (or n) to π * of CC, CN or CO. The broadly visible absorption bands between 520 and 620 nm were related to the partial conjugated π electron in the layered NCDs [33]. For pure g-C3N4, the characteristic spectrum shows a fundamental absorption edge at 470 nm, corresponding to band gap energy of 2.7 eV, which is great agreement with the previous reports [26]. The as-prepared α-Fe2O3 show a broad absorption in visible region and a absorption edge at 627 nm, both of which confirms the excellent visible light absorption ability. The composites of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 show the characteristic absorption bands belong to α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, indicating its outstanding capacity of light absorption. Similarly, the sample of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 have three absorption bands, which are attributed to the components characterized. However, except of a stronger UV absorption, the photocatalyst of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 showed lower absorption in most visible region than α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4, which be ascribed to the covering of NCDs hindered visible light harvesting of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4. The lower capacity of absorbing visible light inevitably reduce degradation efficiency.

The catalytic performance of as-prepared photocatalyst was evaluated by testing the degradation of indole (concentration 50 mg/L, 100 ml), due to its industrial importance and ecotoxicological effects [34]. Firstly, a series of experiments under different conditions (including catalysts, visible light, and H2O2) were performed to identify the specific role in removal reaction ( Fig. 6(a)). It can be obviously observed that degradation of indole in presence of H2O2 was almost negligible (only 7%) in 150 min, which suggest that H2O2 itself barely degrade indole without assistence of any catalysts or light. However, with irradation of light, the removal efficiency were improved upto 30% in presence of H2O2. The improvement may be attributed to the product of •OH from part H2O2 activated by ultraviolet ray in stimulated light [35]. Although the degradation efficiency has slightly arised, the removal ability was still moderate. The degradation efficiency of indole were enhanced to 50% in 150 min with assistence of H2O2 and the as-prepared catalysts of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 in darkness, which is attributed to the existence of Fe(Ⅱ) in the photocatalysts. During the preparation of photocatalyst, iron ions mainly formed the solid α-Fe2O3 on surface of g-C3N4, whose state existing in active site was Fe(Ⅲ). It is inevitable that some iron ions formed iron oxide with state of Fe(Ⅱ), which can activate H2O2 decomposition into •OH radicals even in darkness. When the α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 were applied with H2O2 and light irradation at the same time, the degradation efficiency of indole were upto 85% in 150 min, indicating that the photocatalysts play a major role in degradation process. Compared with other heterogeneous photo-Fenton reactions, this photocatalyst of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 show obvious advantages in degardation efficiency or applicable conditions [34]. It’s worth noting that the total degradation efficiency of indole is not a simple superposition of the efficiency of partial efficiency. This result suggest that there is a synergistic effect between heterogeneous Fenton reaction and photocatalytic degradation, which will be further investigated in section of mechanism analysis.

In addition, the influence of each component in composited photocatalysts on degradation efficiency of indole were also investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 6(b). It can be found that the catalysts of NCDs and composited NCDs/g-C3N4 have no effect on degradation of indole, because both them are lack of iron active site to decompose H2O2 molecule. It can be found that pure α-Fe2O3 has a poor removal efficiency on degradation of indole, about 53% in 150 min. It acted not only as iron source (in active site) to decompose H2O2 into •OH radicals, but also as a semiconductor absorbing light for excitation and separation of electron-hole pairs. The photo-excited electrons transferred and migrated to iron active site through internal or interfacial conductive channels and reduced Fe(Ⅲ) to Fe(Ⅱ) in active site, which accelerated the Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) recycles of solid iron species. Although α-Fe2O3 was able to degrade indole, the removal efficiency was limited by low light absorption ability and high electron-hole recombination. The strategy of combined with other semiconductors is mainstream method to alleviate these limitations. The g-C3N4, as a famous photocatalytic semiconductor, is an ideal composited material due to a two-dimensional planner structure, large surface area, and special light absorption edge. After combined with g-C3N4, the obtained photocatalyst of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 showed a slight higher degradation efficiency of 61% under the same conditions. This slight improvement fails to achieve the expected results, which is owing to weak interfacial contact between components. The lattice mismatch, little contact, interfacial boundaries and defects affect charges separation and migration, leading to poor transfer and migration of charge and further low photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Thus, the NCDs as charges “bridge” was introduced into the composited α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 to overcome above drawbacks. The as-prepared α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 shows an obvious improvement on degradation efficiency of indole, which was up to 83% under the same conditions, which can be attributed to the introduction of NCDs in composite photocatalyst. The small size made NCDs easily to enter the gaps between interfaces of α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, and the rich surface groups facilitate to form more chemical bonds with main semiconductors during the fabrication, which can be considered as a remedy of lattice mismatch or boundary defects. The formed chemical bonds linked or bridged the adjacent semiconductors, which not only enhanced interfacial contact but also constructed the channel between interfaces for charges transfer. Through the chemical bonds and the sp2 hybridized carbon core of NCDs, the transfer and migration of photo-induced charges were significantly improved, resulting in higher degradation efficiencies than any other samples.

The mass percentage of each component has great effect on strucuture-activity relationship for the as-prepared photocatalysts. Thus, the degradation efficiency and reaction rates of catalysts with different mass percentages were tested and the results were shown in Fig.S1(Supported information). In this set of experiments, methylene blue (MB) was selected as target pollutant due to facile operations and representativity in typical pollutants. We roughly tune the ratio of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 by controlling the ration of adding dosage (FeCl3·H2O: NCDS: g-C3N4). The removal efficiencies and pseudo-first-order kinetics by photocatalysts loaded on different mass of α-Fe2O3 are shown in Fig.S1 (a,b). It can be found that all photocatalysts display higher removal efficiencies, which are more than 80% under light irradation for 80 min. In particularly, with increase of α-Fe2O3 loading, the removal activity of composite catalysts first increased and then decreased. The sample with mass percent of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 (3:1:1) showed the highest efficiency of 100% in 80 min and the highest reaction rate constant of 0.0624 min−1, which is the best choice for the adding ratio of α-Fe2O3. When the ratio is lower than this value, more α-Fe2O3 loading meant that more Fe activate sites could involve in degradation processes. However, excessive α-Fe2O3 may provoke non-desirable side reaction and reduce generation of •OH radicals, and thus decreased the degradation efficiency. Fig.S1 (c, d) showed the influence of NCDs mass in the composite photocatalysts on degardation efficiencies and its reaction rate constants. For the photocatalyst in absence of NCDs, the degradation efficiency only attained 70% in 80 min and reaction rate was 0.015 min−1. Both of them were the lowest in all samples, indicating its important effect in the hybrid catalysts. In addition, higher percentage of NCDs in the composites also decreased degradation efficiency of MB. The reasons were that excessive NCDs (with small size) covered some Fe active sites on surface and blocked pore hindering release of •OH radicals. In last, the influence of g-C3N4 percentage in composite catalysts on removal efficiency and reaction rate were shown in Fig.S1 (e, f). The composite catalyst in absence of g-C3N4 show 72% efficiency in 80 min, with reaction rate constant of 0.016. The optimal percentage of g-C3N4 corresponding to about 100% efficiency existed between (3:1:1) to (3:1:2). When the ratio exceeded this value, the degradation efficiency and reaction rate slightly decreased. Excessive g-C3N4 may cover the active sites of α-Fe2O3, which is disadvantageous to the degradation activity.

The experimental conditions of degardation (including photocatalysts amount, H2O2 dosage, initial contaminant concentration and pH value) were performed to assess the influence on degradation of indole ( Fig. 7). Generally, the adding amount of the photocatalysts directly affect activate H2O2 decomposition, and Fig. 7(a) show the degradation efficiencies under different amount of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 (ranging from 0.10 to 0.25 g/L) added in reaction. It was easily observed that the amount between 0.10 and 0.20 g/L resulted in almost equivalent degradation efficiency. Too high or too low amount decreased the degradation efficiency, which is attributed that the excessive iron species consumed some •OH radicals and the lack iron species cannot fully decomposed H2O2 in solution. The influence of H2O2 dosage on degradation efficiencies is exhibited in Fig. 7(b). In general, all dosages possessed more than 60% degradation efficiency in 150 min. The efficiency increased from 62% to 82% with the increase of H2O2 dosage from 45 mmol/L to 90 mmol/L. The improvement of degradation is attributed to the increased amount of •OH radicals at an increasing H2O2 dosage. However, when the dosage of H2O2 increased to 135 mmol/L, the degradation efficiency gradually decreased, which was due to surplus H2O2 molecules acting as scavengers of •OH radicals and producing •OOH or other radicals with lower oxidation potential [36,37]. The initial concentration of contaminant is another important factor to influence on degradation processes. As shown in Fig. 7(c), when the initial indole concentration increased from 20 mg/L to 50 mg/L, overall degradation efficiency has mainly decreased by about 20% in range of 20–50 mg/L. With increase of indole concentration, the decrease efficiency was ascribed to the limitation of the number of reaction sites on the surface of photocatalyst. Fig. 7(d) shows the results of influence of pH value on degradation efficiency. The as-prepared photocatalysts showed a high degradation efficiency in range of 3.2–7.0, in which at least 81% indole were removed in 150 min, indicating that α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 have a wide applicable pH range. There was a decrease of degradation efficiency to 70% at pH= 8.8, which is associated with self-decompositions of H2O2 molecules and weak oxidation potential of •OH radicals at higher pH (E0 =+2.8 V and +2.0 V at pH=0 and 14, respectively) [38].

The enhanced degradation efficiency of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 inspires us to investigate possible mechanism. It is well known that light harvesting ability, behaviors of charges transfer and migration, and energy level alignment of composite photocatalysts are predominant scientific factors for better photocatalytic performance. Hence, it is necessary to study and explore the intrinsic relation between these scientific issues and the degradation efficiency of pollutant. At first, the light harvesting ability of the composites has been measured and discussed in above sections. As shown in Fig. 5, the composite α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 did not have higher light harvesting ability than α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 in range of visible light (major part of solar), suggesting that it have no or little contribution to degradation in section of light harvesting.

Subsequently, the separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs and both charges transfer behaviors of photocatalysts were investigated by photoelectrochemical techniques including transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and the results were shown in Fig. 8(a, b), respectively. It can be observed in Fig. 8(a) that g-C3N4, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 showed stable photocurrent cycles under on-off light irradiation cycles. The α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 have the highest photocurrent density of 5.68 μA/cm2 in all samples, which was 18.93 and 2.37 times than that of g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4, respectively. The α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 possessed higher photocurrent density than bare g-C3N4, which was associated with the well-engineered bandgap arrangement, facilitating both charges separation and transfer through internal electric field. The sample of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 showed the highest photocurrent density, which was attributed to not only the bandgap engineering, but more importantly owing to the introduction of NCDs in the composite. Due to various surface groups, some NCDs reacted with g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3 at the same time during the fabrication of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 and formed chemical bonds such as C-N, Fe-C-O and so on, as revealed by XPS and FTIR spectra. On the one hand, these bonds enhance interfacial contact of adjacent semiconductors. On the other hand, they acting as a bridge linked g-C3N4 with α-Fe2O3, which is considered as a charge channel for transfer between different semiconductors [4]. Thus, these chemical bonds facilitated charges transfer and migration between interfaces of different semiconductors. Moreover, NCDs contain sp2 hybridized carbon core, which are rich in delocalized π electron, leading to great internal conductivity. The surface chemical bonds and internal conductivity of NCDs build up a channel for charge transfer or migration between α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, by which the separation efficiency of electron-holes pairs was enhanced and the transfer path and lifetime of charges were prolonged. Besides, many reports have also demonstrated that CDs remarkably facilitated transfer and migration of electron-hole pairs as electron acceptor or donor in photocatalytic and photoelectronic applications [5,11]. Hence, the channel build by NCDs is responsible to the highest of photocurrent density of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 in all samples. EIS technique was used for further insights into charge transfer behaviors of g-C3N4, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 8(b), α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 show much smaller arc radius than other two samples, which is well agreement with previous report [39,40], indicating the lowest charge transfer resistance during photo-excited process. This result further demonstrate that the introduction of NCDs endow α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 more efficient photo-induced electron-hole separation and faster interfacial charge transfer behaviors.

Besides photoelectronic behaviors, the type of active species formed in reaction is crucial to elucidate degradation process and photocatalytic mechanism. In general, hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (•O2
) and photo-induced hole (h +) are well known to be important ROS involved in degradation of organic pollutants. Thus, IPA, TEOA and BQ were used as •OH, h +and •O2
quenching scavengers in trapping experiments, respectively. The trapping experiments of ROS were performed by adding these scavengers, respectively, and the degradation results were shown in Fig. 9(a). After adding IPA, the degradation efficiency of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 on MB showed a massive decrease from 99% to 10% in 90 min, indicating that •OH was dominant active species in degradation. For scavenger of TEOA, removal efficiency decreased from 99% to 30% under the same conditions, which suggest that h + is another ROS. The degradation efficiency of IPA is 20% higher than that of TEOA, which suggest that •OH is superior to h + in degradation. However, the degradation efficiency was hardly affected by BQ, slightly decreasing by 10%. It is indicative that •O2
radicals were negligibly involved in degradation process. To further demonstrate the reactive oxide species formed in the degradation process, the ESR experiments were carried out with DMPO as spin-trapping reagent. As shown in Fig. 9(b), no obvious signals were recorded for the DMPO-•OH adducts systems in darkness, indicating that no heterogeneous photo-Fenton reaction occurred. Under light irradiation for 5 min, four characteristic signals of the DMPO-•OH with nearly relative intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 can be obviously observed, suggesting the generation of •OH during degradation reaction. However, no DMPO-•O2
adducts were detected under the under the same conditions, indicating the absence of •O2
radical in degradation process, which was well agreement with the results of trapping experiments. The results of trapping experiment and spin-trapping ESR demonstrate that •OH and h + are major ROS for α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 photocatalysts and play a major role in degradation, while •O2
active species was absent in the process. It is well known that •OH radicals stem from the Fenton-like reaction and h + active species usually originate from the separation of electron-hole pairs in photocatalytic process. Hence, the degradation process involved in α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 can be considered the synergy of photocatalytic degradation and heterogeneous photo-Fenton reaction.

According to above experimental results, a possible heterogeneous photo-Fenton mechanism of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 was proposed for decomposing H2O2 into •OH. Fig. 10 shows the scheme of photo-induced charge transfer and migration in photocatalytic process. Due to their narrow bandgap energies located in visible light range, both g-C3N4 and α-Fe2O3 were excited to generate separation of electron-hole pairs (electron (e) in the conduction band (CB) and hole (h+) in the valence band (VB)). According previous literatures [41], the CB and VB potentials position of g-C3N4 are − 1.10 eV and + 1.6 eV (vs. NHE (normal hydrogen electrode)), while the CB and VB of α-Fe2O3 are usually + 0.3 eV and + 2.4 eV (vs. NHE), respectively. Based on these values, there might be two types of band energy alignment (Type Ⅱ and Z-scheme heterojunction) established in the composite, corresponding to two possibly proposed mechanisms on electron-hole pair separation and migration. If a type Ⅱ heterojunction was formed, the photo-induced holes will tend to transfer from VB of α-Fe2O3 to VB of g-C3N4, while the electrons will diffuse to CB of α-Fe2O3 from CB of g-C3N4. Combining with the trapping experiments, the absence of •O2
species can be attributed to the lower CB position of α-Fe2O3 than the redox potential of O2/•O2
(−0.33 eV) [42] and the existence of •OH originating from heterogeneous photo-Fenton reaction. However, the photo-induced holes accumulate on the VB of g-C3N4 were not sufficiently positive to form •OH radical, due to the redox potential of -OH/•OH (+1.98 eV) and H2O/•OH (+2.27 eV) more positive than the VB value of g-C3N4 (+1.60 eV) [43,44]. Hence, the photo-induced holes could not directly oxidize and further degrade contaminant. This conclusion contradicts the results of above scavenger trapping experiments and spin-trapping experiments, in which the holes is an important active species.

Considering the discussion above, the enhanced degradation efficiency of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 system can be ascribed to a Z-scheme carriers transfer mechanism. Under irradiation of light, partial photo-induced electron in CB of α-Fe2O3 tended to transfer to the VB of g-C3N4 and then quickly recombined with the holes in the VB of g-C3N4. Moreover, the VB position of α-Fe2O3 (+2.4 eV) was positive than the redox potential of -OH/•OH (+1.98 eV) and H2O/•OH (+2.27 eV) [45]. Thus, the accumulated holes in the VB of α-Fe2O3 could oxidized –OH and H2O to obtain •OH radicals. Meanwhile, the CB position of α-Fe2O3 (+0.30 eV) is more negative than the standard potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ (+0.77 eV) [32], which endow electron of α-Fe2O3 the ability to accelerate Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) recycles. The photo-induced electron accumulated in the CB of α-Fe2O3 could transfer to Fe(Ⅲ) active sites of solid α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, which were further reduced to Fe(Ⅱ) active site. The produced Fe(Ⅱ) site would further decomposed H2O2 into •OH, which take a dominant role in the degradation of organic contaminants. Both above hypothesis on the form mechanism of •OH and h+ species are well agreement with the results of scavengers trapping and spin-trapping experiments. It is worth noting that the radicals of •O2
should appear in the degradation process, in theory, because the CB of g-C3N4 (−1.1 eV) is more negative than the redox potential of O2/•O2
(−0.33 eV), which resulted in the excited electron spontaneously transition to the dissolved O2 molecules in the solution and the further formation of •O2
species. However, the trapping experiments of scavenger BQ negligibly showed decrease of degradation efficiency, and the •O2
radicals were also not detected in the spin-trapping experiments. Both experimental results were indicative that very few •O2
radicals were formed in the degradation process.

In order to highlight a reasonable mechanism behind the absence of •O2
radical, the effect of NCDs in the composite of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 should further investigated on energy band alignment in details. As well known that carbon dots have another important ability acting as electron acceptor or donor in photocatalytic or photoelectronic applications, which can remarkably improve performance by facilitating separation of electron-hole pairs and charges migration [7,11]. Besides, as a common modification method, nitrogen doping can effectively improve the photocatalytic properties of CDs by lowering work function of carbon and tuning lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level. UPS was performed to explore the energy levels of NCDs to provide experimental basis for the mechanism explanation and the results were shown in Fig.S2 (Supported information). The ionization potential (equivalent to the valance band energy (Ev or LUMO)) of NCDs was calculated to be 7.1 eV (vs. vacuum level) by subtracting the width of the spectra from the excitation energy (21.22 eV). The conduction band energy Ec (or highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)) is thus estimated at − 3.88 eV from Ev-Eg (Energy gap). The Ec, Ev and Eg values of NCDs in electron volts are converted to electro-chemical energy potentials in volts according to the reference standard for which 0 V versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) equals − 4.44 eV versus evac (vacuum level). Therefore, the Ec of NCDs were − 3.88 eV (vs vacuum level) and − 0.56 eV (vs NHE), respectively, which is well agreement with previous report [46]. This energy level is more positive than the CB of g-C3N4 (−1.1 eV vs. NHE), while it is more negative than the redox potential of O2/•O2
(−0.30 eV vs. NHE). This appropriate LUMO level endowed NCDs ability to accept the photo-induced electron from the CB of g-C3N4. Meanwhile, its good conductivity and electron “bridge” formed by chemical bonds facilitated internal transportation and migration between interfaces of electron. Thus, the photo-induced electrons accumulated in the CB of g-C3N4 were prior to jump to closer level (LUMO level of NCDs). Subsequently, they were transmitted to α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles for the acceleration of Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) recycles through the greatly conductive electronic path. Finally, more photo-induced electron in the VB of g-C3N4 were transmitted to the solid α-Fe2O3 and were used to photocatalytically accelerated Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) recycles. The regeneration of Fe(Ⅱ) active site took a predominant part in decomposing H2O2 into •OH radicals. In contrary, due to lack of access to the photo-induces electron from the CB of g-C3N4 and potential barrier at solid-liquid interface, the dissolved oxygen molecule in solution cannot obtain the electron and further generate active species of •O2
radicals. This was the reason why •O2
radicals cannot be detected in the trapping and spin-trapping experiments.

To explore the stability and reusability of the α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4, recycles experiments were carried out by degrading MB. As illustrated in Fig. 11 (a), there was a slight decrease in degradation through each cycle comparing with the first reaction, but there was still 90.5% removal in the fifth cycle. This implies that the composite retains a good reusability undergoing five cycle’s reaction. The slight decrease in catalytic performance is unavoidable, which is owing to the difficulty in full recovery of the spent catalyst and pore blockage on the catalyst surface caused by pollutants and residual intermediates [47]. The stability of spent catalyst is the most directly reflected in the change of crystal structure, which was analyzed by XRD technique and shown in Fig. 11 (b). It can be observed that the XRD patterns of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 after five runs were completely consistent with fresh one, indicating that no obvious changes of lattice structure occurred. Besides, the diffraction peaks at 33.1° and 35.5° of the used catalyst (corresponding to the (104) and (110) diffraction planes, respectively) exhibit even stronger intensity than the fresh one. This phenomenon is attributed to the destruction of some NCDs attached on α-Fe2O3 by •OH, which lead to more exposure of corresponding crystal face. As these results, it could be demonstrated that the catalyst possesses excellent stability.

A composite photocatalyst of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 for heterogeneous photo-Fenton was synthesized by the method of first hydrothermal and then calcination. The TEM images revealed that NCDs and α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles were distributed over the g-C3N4 plane. The XRD patterns and Raman spectra of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 were indicative that three components have successfully hybridized and mainly kept stable in crystal structure. The presence of some important chemical bonds (such as Fe-C-O, C-NC, N-(C)3 and so on) or functional groups (C-N, CN) have been demonstrated in the results of XPS and FTIR spectra. Due to small size, the NCDs attached or deposited on the surface of α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 or the pores existing between α-Fe2O3 and g-C3N4, which not only affected the adsorption and transferring of contaminant molecules, but also decreased the absorption ability in the visible light range. The composite photocatalyst of α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 showed dramatic degradation ability to contaminant due to the synergy of photocatalytic effect and heterogeneous Fenton-like reaction. Based on the degradation efficiencies obtained on experiments, the optimal components ratio was 3:1:2 and the best conditions were catalyst amount of 0.15 g/L, H2O2 dosage of 135 mmol/L, initial contaminant concentration of 50 mg/L and pH values in range of 2.5–5. The photocurrent and EIS analysis showed the increased electron transferring ability and decreased resistance through the composite which is direct evidence of the better photocatalytic activity and high degradation efficiency. The trapping experiments of active species demonstrated that •OH and h + are the main radicals in degradation process, both of which significantly improved the degradation efficiency. The improved mechanism was proposed that the internal electronic field of Z-scheme α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4 drive electron-hole pair separation and charges transferring between interfaces. The introduction of NCDs with great conductive and appropriate energy band facilitated the photo-induced electron in the CB of g-C3N4 migrated to Fe (Ⅲ) active site, which was then reduced to Fe(Ⅱ). The accelerated Fe(Ⅲ)/Fe(Ⅱ) cycles in heterogeneous Fenton-like catalysts significantly improved the generation of •OH radicals and final degradation efficiency. In addition, the accumulated holes in the VB of α-Fe2O3 directly oxidized –OH and H2O to •OH radicals, which also contributed to the improvements of degradation efficiency. The easy reusability and high stability of the α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 allowed its applications several times without any significant decrease in the degradation capacity.

Xiaotong Zhou and Xiaobing Li are responsible for project administration, supervision and writing review draft. Xuehua Li did main experiments and wrote original draft. Fuxiang Ge and Hui Ding did some experiments.
